title: The Second Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## January 17, 2021 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Second Sunday after the Epiphany in the Christian calendar, falling within the season after Epiphany (December 25 through early February). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would typically include Isaiah 49:1-7 (the Servant of the Lord), Psalm 40:1-11, 1 Corinthians 1:1-9 (Paul's greeting to Corinth), and John 1:29-42 (John the Baptist's testimony and the calling of the first disciples). The liturgical emphasis remains on the manifestation of Christ and the calling of disciples to follow him.

---

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the aftermath of General Convention 2018's decisions, still processing internal debates about sexuality, marriage, and church polity. The Diocese of Colorado was led by Bishop Robert Wright, who had been serving since 2011 and was known for progressive stances on LGBTQ+ inclusion. No major denominational crises or elections were immediately pending in January 2021, though the church remained divided nationally over ongoing theological questions and pandemic-era worship practices.

---

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was under Winter Weather Advisories in mid-January 2021, with snow forecast for the week of January 17-19 affecting road conditions and city operations. Local attention remained focused on the aftermath of the January 6 Capitol riot, given Colorado's significant representation of Trump supporters and the state's role in the 2020 election. Denver's Capitol Hill neighborhood, where Saint John's Cathedral is located, was experiencing the typical winter challenges of a downtown urban area during the COVID-19 pandemic's second surge.

---

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

Donald Trump's second impeachment was underway in Congress, with the House having voted to impeach him on January 13 for "incitement of insurrection" following the January 6 Capitol riot; the Senate trial was set to begin the following week. COVID-19 deaths in the United States had exceeded 400,000, with vaccination campaigns ramping up nationwide but facing supply and distribution challenges. Joe Biden's inauguration was scheduled for January 20, 2021, three days after this sermon, dominating national media coverage and raising security concerns following the Capitol breach.

---

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Myanmar was in the opening days of a military coup, with the armed forces deposing civilian leader Aung San Suu Kyi on February 1 (though tensions were already visible to observers in mid-January). The global COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating, with new variant strains (Alpha variant) driving increased case counts across Europe and other regions. Israeli-Palestinian tensions were elevated in early 2021, following Gaza airstrikes and Palestinian rocket fire in the previous month.

---

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in a state of acute political and social anxiety, still reeling from the January 6 Capitol riot and deeply divided along partisan lines heading into Biden's inauguration. The broader cultural mood was one of pandemic fatigue mixed with hope that vaccination might provide an exit from COVID-19 restrictions; churches and gatherings remained cautious or virtual in many areas. The Denver Broncos had finished their NFL season poorly (5-11 record), and winter sports were ongoing, but national cultural attention was almost entirely consumed by political upheaval and the transition of presidential power.
